title: Uniqueness of the contact structure approximating a foliation
speaker: Thomas Vogel
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Existence and uniqueness theorems with hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by recalling the Eliashberg-Thurston approximation theorem for C^2 foliations and stating the speaker's uniqueness theorem: under no-torus-leaf and non-plane/non-cylinder hypotheses, all sufficiently close positive contact structures are pairwise isotopic.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions motivating the exceptional hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: Examples on T^3 show how torus leaves and irrational plane foliations defeat uniqueness or deformation, and introduce pre-Lagrangian tori and sheets as the mechanism for detecting the isotopy class of nearby contact structures.

proof_stages.label: Minimal sets and the C^2 holonomy input
proof_stages.summary: The C^2 assumption is tied to the structure of minimal sets, especially Sacksteder-type attractive holonomy in exceptional minimal sets, which supplies the dynamical control absent in certain C^0 foliations.

proof_stages.label: C^0 pathology and speculative replacement hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: A C^0 foliation built from a holonomy-free geodesic lamination on Sigma x S^1 is taut and contact-approximable but has cylinder minimal sets without holonomy, suggesting that naive C^0 uniqueness should fail unless such no-holonomy plane or cylinder minimal sets are excluded.

proof_stages.label: Eliashberg-Thurston local contactization and propagation
proof_stages.summary: The approximation theorem is sketched by modifying the plane field in a box around a curve with attractive holonomy to create a confoliation/contact region, then propagating nonintegrability along Legendrian curves from neighborhoods of the minimal sets.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of uniqueness to a product neighborhood
proof_stages.summary: For the illustrative case of a single hyperbolic closed minimal leaf, two nearby contact structures are arranged, after isotopy and choice of a transverse line field, to agree outside a neighborhood modeled on the leaf times an interval.

proof_stages.label: Honda-Kazez-Matic classification input
proof_stages.summary: The remaining comparison is reduced to classifying tight contact structures on a surface times an interval with fixed boundary germ and controlled convex boundary/dividing curves, yielding only finitely many possible extensions.

proof_stages.label: Eliminating extra dividing annuli using closeness
proof_stages.summary: By moving the product surfaces to remove all but one attractive closed curve while using C^0-closeness to keep the moves inside the controlled neighborhood, the classification hypotheses are achieved and the correct extension is identified, completing the uniqueness strategy.

examples.name: Product foliation on T^3 with Giroux-torsion approximations
examples.note: The foliation ker(dz) on T^3 is approximated by contact forms depending on a winding parameter k, showing directly that torus leaves allow many nonisotopic nearby contact structures.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly sets up the product foliation on T^3 as ker dz, writes a two-parameter contact-form approximation, checks its limiting behavior as epsilon goes to zero, and identifies the Giroux torsion as k-1.

passes.reason: The translation-invariant product torus foliation with Giroux-torsion contact approximations is a highly regular model used to show torus leaves destroy uniqueness.
It is the trivial product foliation by level tori, used to show torus leaves allow non-isotopic Giroux-torsion approximations and hence non-uniqueness.
the translation-invariant product torus foliation is used with different Giroux-torsion approximations to refute uniqueness when torus leaves are allowed
translation-invariant product foliation whose Giroux-torsion approximations show non-uniqueness when torus leaves are allowed
A translation-invariant product torus foliation with standard Giroux-torsion contact approximations, used to show torus leaves destroy uniqueness.
the trivial product torus foliation is used to show torus leaves destroy uniqueness via distinct Giroux-torsion approximations
the translation-invariant product foliation by torus leaves is a simple degenerate model used to show that varying Giroux torsion destroys uniqueness.
The product torus foliation is a trivial translation-invariant fibration used to show torus leaves destroy uniqueness via approximations with different Giroux torsion.
Shows torus leaves allow nonisotopic Giroux-torsion approximations, using a flat translation-invariant product foliation.
the translation-invariant product torus foliation is used to show torus leaves allow non-isotopic Giroux-torsion contact approximations and hence failure of uniqueness.
It is used to show torus leaves destroy uniqueness, and the product/linear foliation on T^3 is highly regular and translation-invariant.
It is used to show torus leaves permit nonunique approximating contact structures with different Giroux torsion, and the product/linear torus model is highly regular.

examples.name: Level pre-Lagrangian tori in T^3
examples.note: Intersecting the T^3 contact structures with level tori gives characteristic foliations by parallel lines, making concrete the notion of pre-Lagrangian tori and their distinguished isotopy class.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker takes a level torus T^2 x {z}, intersects its tangent planes with the contact structure, derives the characteristic foliation by parallel lines, and discusses when its leaves are closed versus noncompact.

passes.reason: The level pre-Lagrangian tori have a regular parallel-line characteristic foliation and are used as a diagnostic/classification feature, not to refute a claim.
These are standard level tori with maximally regular linear characteristic foliations, used as an invariant rather than to refute a claim.
the level tori have a very regular parallel characteristic foliation and are used as an invariant rather than as a disproof
level tori with parallel linear characteristic foliations are regular T3 model objects used as identifying tools, not to refute a claim
The level tori are flat pre-Lagrangian tori with parallel characteristic foliations, used as an invariant rather than to refute a claim.
the level tori have maximally regular linear characteristic foliations and serve as an invariant rather than a failure example
the level tori have completely regular parallel characteristic foliations and are introduced as standard pre-Lagrangian tori rather than as an obstruction.
The level tori are regular pre-Lagrangian slices with linear characteristic foliations, used as a diagnostic for contact structures rather than to refute a claim.
Used as regular linear pre-Lagrangian tori singled out by the T^3 contact structure, not to refute a claim.
the level tori are flat regular pre-Lagrangian tori with parallel characteristic foliations, used illustratively as a contact-topological invariant rather than as a failure case.
They are used as identifying features of contact structures, with maximally regular parallel characteristic foliations on flat level tori.
The level tori are illustrative pre-Lagrangian tori with parallel-line characteristic foliations, a regular flat model rather than an obstruction.

examples.name: Irrational linear foliation by planes on T^3
examples.note: A linear foliation with irrational plane leaves has contact structures in every C^0-neighborhood but no fixed isotopy class accumulating on it, motivating the exclusion of plane foliations and the sheet/pre-Lagrangian detection method.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines the irrational linear foliation by a rationally independent plane field and then works through the obstruction argument using transverse torus leaves, characteristic foliations, pre-Lagrangian tori, and angle bounds.

passes.reason: The irrational linear plane foliation is a homogeneous Kronecker-type foliation used to show that approximation need not occur within any fixed contact-isotopy class or deformation.
It is a homogeneous linear foliation by the simplest open leaves, deployed to show deformation/uniqueness can fail for the all-plane case.
the homogeneous irrational linear plane foliation is used to show that no fixed contact-isotopy component can deform or accumulate to it
translation-invariant irrational plane foliation is used to show a fixed contact isotopy class need not accumulate and deformation can fail
A homogeneous irrational linear foliation by dense planes on T^3, used to show a foliation need not deform into a contact structure in any fixed isotopy class.
the homogeneous irrational plane foliation is used to show deformation/uniqueness can fail in the all-plane case
the irrational linear plane foliation is translation-invariant and is used to show that no fixed contact isotopy class can deform or accumulate to it.
The irrational Kronecker foliation is a homogeneous dense-plane foliation used to show that approximation need not arise from a deformation/fixed contact isotopy class.
Used to show fixed contact isotopy classes cannot accumulate to the foliation, and the irrational linear all-plane case is a translation-invariant edge case.
the homogeneous irrational linear foliation has all leaves dense planes and is used to show approximation need not come from a deformation or fixed contact isotopy class.
It obstructs deformation into a fixed contact isotopy class, while the irrational linear foliation is homogeneous and translation-invariant.
The irrational linear plane foliation is used to show deformation into a fixed contact isotopy class can fail, while its all-plane leaves are minimal-topology and the model is homogeneous.

examples.name: Three minimal-set types for foliations
examples.note: Closed leaves, the whole manifold in the dense-leaf case, and exceptional Cantor-transversal sets illustrate the minimal-set alternatives used to explain where the C^2 hypothesis enters.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the minimal-set trichotomy by unpacking what each type looks like, especially via transverse arcs and the Cantor-set/holonomy behavior of exceptional minimal sets.

passes.reason: Minimal sets are by definition minimal nonempty closed saturated subsets, and the trichotomy is used explanatorily rather than adversarially.
Minimal sets are inclusion-minimal closed saturated sets, and the three-type list is used to organize the proof rather than as an adversarial example.
minimal sets are inclusion-minimal closed saturated subsets, and the trichotomy is explanatory rather than adversarial
minimal sets are by definition inclusion-minimal closed saturated subsets, and the trichotomy is explanatory rather than adversarial
This is a general trichotomy of minimal sets, and the foliation-theoretic 'exceptional' Cantor case is not a sporadic classification exception.
the three minimal-set cases are a general taxonomy used explanatorily, with “exceptional” not meaning a sporadic classification exception
minimal sets are by definition inclusion-minimal closed saturated subsets, and the trichotomy is used as explanatory structure rather than to refute a claim.
The closed-leaf and whole-manifold cases are boundary cases in the minimal-set trichotomy, introduced as setup rather than as an obstruction.
Invoked as the standard taxonomy of minimal sets, which are extremal closed saturated subsets by inclusion rather than sporadic exceptions.
this is a standard trichotomy of possible minimal sets used for exposition, and the foliation term exceptional is not a sporadic classification exception.
This is a heterogeneous trichotomy of possible minimal sets rather than one classifiable object.
This is a background trichotomy for minimal sets, not an adversarial object, and 'exceptional' here is foliation terminology rather than a sporadic classification exception.

examples.name: C^0 foliation from a geodesic lamination on Sigma x S^1
examples.note: Lifting a holonomy-free geodesic lamination to cylinders in Sigma x S^1 and filling the complementary even-sided regions gives a taut C^0 foliation whose nearby contact structures should vary by approximating curve systems, suggesting extra exclusions needed for any C^0 uniqueness theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the C^0 foliation from a geodesic lamination on Sigma x S^1, explains the even-sided polygon filling, describes the resulting leaves and minimal sets, and reasons why it is not homeomorphic to a C^2 foliation.

passes.reason: The C^0 foliation built from a holonomy-free geodesic lamination is a non-sporadic pathological model used against a naive C^0 uniqueness analogue.
The lamination-built C^0 foliation is a non-sporadic low-regularity construction used to challenge a naive C^0 uniqueness analogue.
the C0 foliation built from a holonomy-free geodesic lamination is presented as an obstruction to extending uniqueness to the C0 category
C0 lamination-built foliation is presented as an obstruction to a naive C0 uniqueness analogue, without being a sporadic exceptional object
A low-regularity boundary C^0 foliation built from a holonomy-free lamination, used as the enemy case for C^0 uniqueness.
the C0 lamination-built foliation is deployed as an obstruction to a naive C0 uniqueness analogue
the only-continuous lamination filling with holonomy-free cylindrical minimal sets is a low-regularity degenerate case used against a naive C0 uniqueness analogue.

The C^0 foliation built from a holonomy-free geodesic lamination is used as an adversarial example to the expected C^0 uniqueness analogue, but it belongs to a systematic construction rather than a sporadic class.
Used as a C^0 obstruction to a naive uniqueness theorem, with non-C^2 low-regularity holonomy-free cylinder minimal sets.
the constructed only-C0 non-smoothable foliation with holonomy-free cylindrical minimal sets is used against a naive C0 uniqueness analogue.
It is presented as an obstruction to extending the uniqueness theorem to C^0 foliations, but the lamination-built foliation is not a sporadic or symmetric object.
The C0 foliation built from a geodesic lamination is deployed as an obstruction to a naive C0 uniqueness theorem, but it is not a sporadic, extremal, or symmetric object.
